Selection}\label{how-aic-guides-model-selection}

When comparing competing regression models, we need a principled way to
decide whether a simpler model is preferable to a more complex one.
Model selection criteria address this problem by balancing goodness of
fit against model complexity, so that additional predictors are retained
only when they improve the model enough to justify their inclusion.

One widely used criterion is the Akaike Information Criterion (AIC). AIC
combines a measure of model fit with a penalty for complexity, with
lower values indicating a better trade-off. For linear regression, AIC
can be expressed, up to an additive constant, as \[
AIC = 2m + n \log\left(\frac{SSE}{n}\right),
\] where \(m\) denotes the number of estimated parameters in the model,
\(n\) is the number of observations, and \(SSE\) is the sum of squared
errors introduced in Equation~\ref{eq-sse}.

Unlike \(R^2\), which never decreases when predictors are added, AIC
explicitly penalizes model complexity. As a result, a predictor is
retained only if the improvement in fit is large enough to justify the
added complexity. AIC is therefore a relative measure: it is meaningful
only when comparing models fitted to the same dataset and response
variable, and among the candidate models, the one with the smaller AIC
is preferred.

A related criterion is the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC), which
penalizes complexity more strongly and therefore tends to favor simpler
models. In this chapter, however, we focus on AIC because it is the
default criterion used by the \texttt{step()} function in R.

\subsection*{\texorpdfstring{Stepwise Regression in Practice: Using
\texttt{step()} in
R}{Stepwise Regression in Practice: Using step() in R}}\label{stepwise-regression-in-practice-using-step-in-r}

We now apply stepwise regression to the \texttt{house} dataset. In R,
the \texttt{step()} function automates predictor selection by
iteratively adding or removing variables to improve the AIC value. Its
general syntax is

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{step}\NormalTok{(object, }\AttributeTok{direction =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"both"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"backward"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"forward"}\NormalTok{))}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Here, \texttt{object} is a fitted model, and the \texttt{direction}
argument specifies the search strategy. Forward selection starts from a
smaller model and adds predictors, backward elimination begins with a
fuller model and removes predictors, and \texttt{"both"} allows movement
in either direction.

For the \texttt{house} data, we begin with the full multiple regression
model introduced earlier in Section \ref{sec-ch10-multiple-regression}.
This model, stored as \texttt{full\_model}, includes all available
predictors of \texttt{unit\_price}. As already noted, some coefficient
estimates may have relatively large \emph{p}-values. That does not
necessarily mean that those predictors are unimportant. When predictors
contain overlapping information, their individual contributions can be
difficult to separate clearly within a single model. This is closely
related to multicollinearity, which can inflate standard errors and
complicate interpretation even when the model as a whole fits the data
well.

This motivates the use of automated model selection techniques. We apply
stepwise regression using AIC as the selection criterion and allowing
both forward and backward moves:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{stepwise\_model }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{step}\NormalTok{(full\_model, }\AttributeTok{direction =} \StringTok{"both"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{   Start}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{  AIC}\OtherTok{=}\FloatTok{1822.01}
\NormalTok{   unit\_price }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{ house\_age }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ distance\_to\_MRT }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ stores\_number }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ latitude }\SpecialCharTok{+} 
\NormalTok{       longitude}
   
\NormalTok{                     Df Sum of Sq   RSS    AIC}
   \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ longitude        }\DecValTok{1}       \FloatTok{2.0} \DecValTok{32792} \FloatTok{1820.0}
   \SpecialCharTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{none}\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}}                         \DecValTok{32790} \FloatTok{1822.0}
   \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ latitude         }\DecValTok{1}    \FloatTok{2248.8} \DecValTok{35038} \FloatTok{1847.5}
   \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ distance\_to\_MRT  }\DecValTok{1}    \FloatTok{2786.3} \DecValTok{35576} \FloatTok{1853.8}
   \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ stores\_number    }\DecValTok{1}    \FloatTok{3004.2} \DecValTok{35794} \FloatTok{1856.3}
   \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ house\_age        }\DecValTok{1}    \FloatTok{3821.9} \DecValTok{36611} \FloatTok{1865.7}
   
\NormalTok{   Step}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{  AIC}\OtherTok{=}\FloatTok{1820.03}
\NormalTok{   unit\_price }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{ house\_age }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ distance\_to\_MRT }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ stores\_number }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ latitude}
   
\NormalTok{                     Df Sum of Sq   RSS    AIC}
   \SpecialCharTok{\textless{}}\NormalTok{none}\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}}                         \DecValTok{32792} \FloatTok{1820.0}
   \SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ longitude        }\DecValTok{1}       \FloatTok{2.0} \DecValTok{32790} \FloatTok{1822.0}
   \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ latitude         }\DecValTok{1}    \FloatTok{2299.3} \DecValTok{35091} \FloatTok{1846.1}
   \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ stores\_number    }\DecValTok{1}    \FloatTok{3023.6} \DecValTok{35815} \FloatTok{1854.5}
   \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ house\_age        }\DecValTok{1}    \FloatTok{3820.2} \DecValTok{36612} \FloatTok{1863.7}
   \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ distance\_to\_MRT  }\DecValTok{1}    \FloatTok{5755.8} \DecValTok{38547} \FloatTok{1885.0}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The algorithm evaluates alternative models by adding or removing
predictors, retaining only those changes that reduce the AIC. This
process continues until no further improvement is possible. Across the
iterations, the AIC decreases from 1822.01 for the full model to 1820.03
for the final selected model, indicating a slightly more favorable
balance between fit and complexity.

To see which predictors remain in the final model, we inspect its
formula:

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{formula}\NormalTok{(stepwise\_model)}
\NormalTok{   unit\_price }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{ house\_age }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ distance\_to\_MRT }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ stores\_number }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ latitude}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The stepwise procedure removes only one predictor, \texttt{longitude}.
This suggests that, after accounting for the other variables in the
model, \texttt{longitude} does not contribute enough additional
information to be retained under the AIC criterion. All other predictors
from the full model remain in the final specification.

Compared with the full model, the stepwise model is only slightly
simpler. Its residual standard error changes from 8.96 to 8.95, while
adjusted \(R^2\) changes from 56.6\% to 56.7\%. These values show that
removing \texttt{longitude} has very little effect on the overall fit of
the model.

Stepwise regression therefore provides a practical way to compare
competing predictor sets, especially when several predictors may contain
overlapping information. At the same time, it remains a heuristic
approach and should be complemented with subject-matter knowledge,
diagnostic checks, and validation whenever possible.

\begin{quote}
\emph{Practice:} Apply stepwise regression using \texttt{"forward"} and
\texttt{"backward"} selection instead of \texttt{"both"}. Do all three
approaches lead to the same final model? How do their AIC values
compare?
\end{quote}

\subsection*{Considerations for Stepwise
Regression}\label{considerations-for-stepwise-regression}

Stepwise regression provides a structured and computationally efficient
approach to predictor selection. By iteratively adding or removing
variables according to a model selection criterion, it offers a
practical way to compare competing models without evaluating every
possible predictor combination. When used carefully, it can produce
simpler and more interpretable models.

At the same time, stepwise regression has important limitations. Because
predictors are evaluated sequentially rather than jointly, the procedure
may miss combinations of variables or interaction effects that become
useful only when considered together. The selected model can also be
sensitive to sampling variability, so small changes in the data may lead
to different results. When many predictors are available relative to the
sample size, stepwise regression may favor models that capture random
noise rather than stable patterns. Multicollinearity can further
complicate interpretation by inflating standard errors and obscuring
individual contributions.

In settings with many predictors or more complex dependence structures,
regularization methods such as LASSO and ridge regression are often
attractive alternatives. These approaches shrink coefficient estimates
through explicit penalty terms and can produce more stable models,
especially when predictors are numerous or highly correlated. A broader
introduction to these methods is provided in \emph{An Introduction to
Statistical Learning with Applications in R} (Gareth et al. 2013).

Ultimately, predictor selection should be guided by a combination of
statistical criteria, subject-matter knowledge, and validation on
representative data. Stepwise regression is therefore best viewed not as
a definitive solution, but as a useful exploratory tool that can support
model building when applied with care.

\section{Modeling Non-Linear
Relationships}\label{modeling-non-linear-relationships}

Not all relationships between predictors and a response variable are
well described by straight lines. In many applications, the effect of a
predictor changes across its range, so that the relationship is curved
rather than linear. When this happens, a standard linear regression
model may miss important structure in the data and produce systematic
errors.

Earlier in this chapter, we used stepwise regression (Section
\ref{sec-ch10-stepwise}) to decide which predictors to retain in the
model. That approach helps refine model specification, but it does not
address the form of the relationship between a predictor and the
response. If the underlying pattern is curved, simply adding or removing
predictors will not solve the problem.

To handle this situation while preserving the familiar regression
framework, we turn to polynomial regression. This approach extends
linear regression by allowing transformed versions of a predictor, such
as squared terms, to enter the model. In this way, it can capture
curvature while retaining the same basic tools for estimation,
interpretation, and prediction.

\subsection*{When a Straight Line Is Not
Enough}\label{when-a-straight-line-is-not-enough}

A useful way to see why non-linear terms may be needed is to examine the
relationship between \texttt{house\_age} and \texttt{unit\_price} in the
\texttt{house} dataset. Figure Figure~\ref{fig-scatter-plot-non-reg}
shows a scatter plot of these two variables. The dashed gray line
represents a simple linear regression fit, while the dark blue curve
represents a quadratic fit.

\begin{figure}[H]

\centering{

\includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{10-Regression_files/figure-pdf/fig-scatter-plot-non-reg-1.pdf}

}

\caption{\label{fig-scatter-plot-non-reg}Scatter plot of
\texttt{unit\_price} versus \texttt{house\_age} in the \texttt{house}
dataset, with a fitted linear regression line shown as a dashed gray
line and a fitted quadratic curve shown in dark blue.}

\end{figure}%

The figure suggests that the relationship is not fully captured by a
straight line. The linear fit follows the overall direction of the data,
but it does not reflect the visible curvature particularly well. In
contrast, the quadratic curve follows the pattern more closely,
suggesting that the effect of \texttt{house\_age} on
\texttt{unit\_price} is not constant across the full range of the
predictor.

A quadratic regression model has the form \[
\widehat{\text{unit\_price}} = b_0 + b_1 \times \text{house\_age} + b_2 \times \text{house\_age}^2.
\]

By including both \texttt{house\_age} and its squared term, the fitted
relationship is allowed to bend rather than remain straight. Although
the relationship between the predictor and the response is now
nonlinear, the model is still linear in the coefficients \(b_0\),
\(b_1\), and \(b_2\). For that reason, it is still estimated using
ordinary least squares.

This plot is intended to build intuition by isolating the relationship
between \texttt{house\_age} and \texttt{unit\_price}. In the next
section, we return to the multivariable regression setting and examine
whether adding a quadratic term for \texttt{house\_age} improves the
earlier model selected from the \texttt{house} data.

\section{Polynomial Regression in
Practice}\label{polynomial-regression-in-practice}

We now return to the \texttt{house} dataset and examine how polynomial
regression can be used in a multivariable setting. In the previous
section, we used the relationship between \texttt{house\_age} and
\texttt{unit\_price} to build intuition for why a straight-line fit may
be insufficient. We now extend that idea to the regression model
selected earlier by the stepwise procedure.

The stepwise model retained the predictors \texttt{house\_age},
\texttt{distance\_to\_MRT}, \texttt{stores\_number}, and
\texttt{latitude}, but it treated the effect of \texttt{house\_age} as
linear. Because the earlier scatter plot suggested curvature in the
relationship between \texttt{house\_age} and \texttt{unit\_price}, it is
natural to ask whether the model improves when we allow this
relationship to bend.

To do so, we add a quadratic term for \texttt{house\_age} while
retaining the other predictors from the stepwise model. The resulting
polynomial regression model is \begin{equation}
\begin{split}
\widehat{\text{unit\_price}} &=
b_0 + b_1 \times \text{house\_age} +
b_2 \times \text{house\_age}^2 +
b_3 \times \text{distance\_to\_MRT} + \\
&\quad b_4 \times \text{stores\_number} +
b_5 \times \text{latitude}.
\end{split}
\end{equation}

This model remains linear in the coefficients, even though the
relationship between \texttt{house\_age} and \texttt{unit\_price} is no
longer restricted to a straight line. We can therefore fit it using the
same \texttt{lm()} function as before.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{poly\_reg\_house }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{lm}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  unit\_price }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{ house\_age }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{I}\NormalTok{(house\_age}\SpecialCharTok{\^{}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ distance\_to\_MRT }\SpecialCharTok{+}
\NormalTok{    stores\_number }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ latitude,}
  \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ house}
\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{summary}\NormalTok{(poly\_reg\_house)}
   
\NormalTok{   Call}\SpecialCharTok{:}
   \FunctionTok{lm}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{formula =}\NormalTok{ unit\_price }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{ house\_age }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{I}\NormalTok{(house\_age}\SpecialCharTok{\^{}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ distance\_to\_MRT }\SpecialCharTok{+} 
\NormalTok{       stores\_number }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ latitude, }\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ house)}
   
\NormalTok{   Residuals}\SpecialCharTok{:}
\NormalTok{       Min      }\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{Q  Median      }\DecValTok{3}\NormalTok{Q     Max }
   \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{31.573}  \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{5.160}  \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{0.753}   \FloatTok{4.309}  \FloatTok{77.758} 
   
\NormalTok{   Coefficients}\SpecialCharTok{:}
\NormalTok{                     Estimate Std. Error t value }\FunctionTok{Pr}\NormalTok{(}\SpecialCharTok{\textgreater{}}\ErrorTok{|}\NormalTok{t}\SpecialCharTok{|}\NormalTok{)    }
\NormalTok{   (Intercept)     }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{6.225e+03}  \FloatTok{1.070e+03}  \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{5.818} \FloatTok{1.20e{-}08} \SpecialCharTok{**}\ErrorTok{*}
\NormalTok{   house\_age       }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{1.102e+00}  \FloatTok{1.446e{-}01}  \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{7.616} \FloatTok{1.84e{-}13} \SpecialCharTok{**}\ErrorTok{*}
   \FunctionTok{I}\NormalTok{(house\_age}\SpecialCharTok{\^{}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)   }\FloatTok{2.090e{-}02}  \FloatTok{3.507e{-}03}   \FloatTok{5.961} \FloatTok{5.44e{-}09} \SpecialCharTok{**}\ErrorTok{*}
\NormalTok{   distance\_to\_MRT }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{3.530e{-}03}  \FloatTok{4.854e{-}04}  \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{7.272} \FloatTok{1.82e{-}12} \SpecialCharTok{**}\ErrorTok{*}
\NormalTok{   stores\_number    }\FloatTok{1.086e+00}  \FloatTok{1.826e{-}01}   \FloatTok{5.945} \FloatTok{5.93e{-}09} \SpecialCharTok{**}\ErrorTok{*}
\NormalTok{   latitude         }\FloatTok{2.512e+02}  \FloatTok{4.284e+01}   \FloatTok{5.863} \FloatTok{9.38e{-}09} \SpecialCharTok{**}\ErrorTok{*}
   \SpecialCharTok{{-}{-}{-}}
\NormalTok{   Signif. codes}\SpecialCharTok{:}  \DecValTok{0} \StringTok{\textquotesingle{}***\textquotesingle{}} \FloatTok{0.001} \StringTok{\textquotesingle{}**\textquotesingle{}} \FloatTok{0.01} \StringTok{\textquotesingle{}*\textquotesingle{}} \FloatTok{0.05} \StringTok{\textquotesingle{}.\textquotesingle{}} \FloatTok{0.1} \StringTok{\textquotesingle{} \textquotesingle{}} \DecValTok{1}
   
\NormalTok{   Residual standard error}\SpecialCharTok{:} \FloatTok{8.598}\NormalTok{ on }\DecValTok{408}\NormalTok{ degrees of freedom}
\NormalTok{   Multiple R}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{squared}\SpecialCharTok{:}  \FloatTok{0.6055}\NormalTok{, Adjusted R}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{squared}\SpecialCharTok{:}  \FloatTok{0.6007} 
\NormalTok{   F}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{statistic}\SpecialCharTok{:} \FloatTok{125.2}\NormalTok{ on }\DecValTok{5}\NormalTok{ and }\DecValTok{408}\NormalTok{ DF,  p}\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{value}\SpecialCharTok{:} \ErrorTok{\textless{}} \FloatTok{2.2e{-}16}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

In the summary output, we see two coefficients related to
\texttt{house\_age}: one for \texttt{house\_age} and one for
\texttt{I(house\_age\^{}2)}. This is because the model includes both the
linear term and the squared term. Together, these two coefficients
determine the curved relationship between \texttt{house\_age} and
\texttt{unit\_price}. As a result, the effect of \texttt{house\_age} can
no longer be interpreted using a single slope alone.

We now compare this polynomial model with the earlier stepwise linear
model. If the quadratic term captures meaningful curvature, we would
expect the polynomial model to improve the fit. For the stepwise linear
model, the residual standard error is 8.95 and the adjusted \(R^2\) is
0.567. For the polynomial model, the residual standard error decreases
to 8.6, while the adjusted \(R^2\) increases to 0.601. The polynomial
model therefore has a lower residual standard error and a higher
adjusted \(R^2\). This suggests that allowing curvature in
\texttt{house\_age} improves the model beyond the purely linear
specification. In other words, the quadratic term appears to capture
structure that the stepwise model leaves unexplained.

Polynomial regression therefore provides a useful extension of multiple
regression when one predictor appears to have a nonlinear relationship
with the response. At the same time, polynomial terms should be added
thoughtfully and only when they are supported by the data and improve
the model in a meaningful way. In the next section, we examine whether
this refinement is also supported by the diagnostic plots.

\begin{quote}
\emph{Practice:} Fit a cubic extension of the model by replacing
\texttt{I(house\_age\^{}2)} with both \texttt{I(house\_age\^{}2)} and
\texttt{I(house\_age\^{}3)}. Compare the cubic and quadratic models
using the residual standard error, adjusted \(R^2\), and the diagnostic
plots. Does the added cubic term appear to improve the model enough to
justify the extra complexity?
\end{quote}

\section{Diagnosing Common Problems in Regression
Models}\label{diagnosing-common-problems-in-regression-models}

A regression model may appear to perform well according to measures such
as \(R^2\), adjusted \(R^2\), or the residual standard error, yet still
suffer from important weaknesses. Before we rely on a fitted model for
interpretation or prediction, we therefore need to examine whether it
shows signs of systematic problems. Diagnostic analysis helps us do this
by revealing patterns in the residuals, highlighting unusual
observations, and showing whether the fitted model captures the
structure of the data adequately.

Linear regression is built on several familiar assumptions, including
linearity, constant variance, approximate normality of residuals, and
independence of observations. In practice, however, it is often more
useful to approach diagnostics through the concrete problems that arise
when these assumptions are not well satisfied. Rather than asking only
whether an assumption holds in the abstract, we ask three practical
questions: What can go wrong, how can we recognize it, and what might we
do in response?

Even when a polynomial model improves numerical fit, it is still
important to check whether the fitted model behaves reasonably. To
illustrate this process, we return to the \texttt{house} dataset and
focus on the polynomial regression model introduced earlier. In that
model, \texttt{unit\_price} is explained using \texttt{house\_age}, its
squared term, \texttt{distance\_to\_MRT}, \texttt{stores\_number}, and
\texttt{latitude}. The standard diagnostic plots for this model are
shown in Figure~\ref{fig-ch10-model-diagnostics}. Here we use the
\texttt{autoplot()} function from \textbf{ggfortify} because it provides
a clean and convenient display of the standard regression diagnostics,
although the same plots can also be obtained using the base
\texttt{plot()} function in R. The lower-right panel additionally
includes Cook's distance contours, which help us identify cases that may
have a relatively strong influence on the fitted model.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{library}\NormalTok{(ggfortify)}

\FunctionTok{autoplot}\NormalTok{(poly\_reg\_house, }\AttributeTok{which =} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{figure}[H]

\centering{

\includegraphics[width=1\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{10-Regression_files/figure-pdf/fig-ch10-model-diagnostics-1.pdf}

}

\caption{\label{fig-ch10-model-diagnostics}Diagnostic plots for
assessing common problems in the fitted polynomial regression model for
the \texttt{house} dataset.}

\end{figure}%

These four plots provide complementary perspectives on model adequacy.
The Residuals vs Fitted plot (upper-left) helps us assess linearity and
constant variance, the Normal Q-Q plot (upper-right) helps us assess
whether the residuals are approximately normal, the Scale-Location plot
(lower-left) provides another view of residual spread, and the Residuals
vs Leverage plot (lower-right) helps us identify observations that may
be influential. Taken together, these plots do not provide automatic
yes-or-no answers, but they help us judge whether the fitted model
behaves reasonably or whether some aspects require closer attention. In
the following subsections, we examine these diagnostic perspectives in
more detail.

One important issue is not well captured by the four standard diagnostic
plots: dependence among observations. Standard linear regression assumes
that each observation contributes independent information, but this may
be questionable in datasets with temporal, spatial, clustered, or
repeated-measurement structure. When dependence is present, the fitted
regression function may still appear reasonable, but the standard errors
and inferential conclusions can become unreliable. For this reason,
regression diagnostics require not only plot-based checks, but also
careful attention to how the data were collected and structured.

In this case, the plots do not suggest any severe violation of the main
regression assumptions, although observation 271 stands out and deserves
closer inspection. The Cook's distance contours in the Residuals vs
Leverage plot help us assess whether this case may also have an
important influence on the fitted model.

\subsection*{Non-Linearity of the Response-Predictor
Relationship}\label{non-linearity-of-the-response-predictor-relationship}

One of the most common problems in regression analysis is non-linearity.
A linear regression model assumes that the response changes linearly
with the predictors included in the model, given the structure specified
by the model. When this assumption is not appropriate, the fitted model
may systematically underpredict in some regions of the data and
overpredict in others.

A useful tool for detecting this problem is the Residuals vs Fitted
plot. If the assumed functional form is adequate, the residuals should
appear randomly scattered around zero with no clear structure. Curved
patterns or other systematic departures suggest that the model is
missing important non-linear structure.

To illustrate this, Figure~\ref{fig-ch10-linearity-comparison} compares
the Residuals vs Fitted plots for two models fitted to the
\texttt{house} dataset: the earlier stepwise linear model
\texttt{stepwise\_model} and the polynomial regression model
\texttt{poly\_reg\_house}. This comparison is especially useful because
it shows not only what non-linearity looks like in practice, but also
how a model can be improved once that pattern is detected. The stepwise
model includes \texttt{house\_age}, \texttt{distance\_to\_MRT},
\texttt{stores\_number}, and \texttt{latitude}, but assumes a linear
effect of \texttt{house\_age}. The polynomial model extends this
specification by adding the squared term \(house\_age^2\).

In the stepwise linear model (left panel), the residuals show a
noticeable curved pattern, suggesting that the model does not fully
capture the relationship between \texttt{house\_age} and
\texttt{unit\_price}. In the polynomial model (right panel), this
pattern is less pronounced, and the residuals appear more randomly
scattered around zero. This suggests that allowing curvature in
\texttt{house\_age} provides a better representation of the underlying
relationship.

\begin{figure}

\centering{

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{autoplot}\NormalTok{(stepwise\_model, }\AttributeTok{which =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{ncol =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{ggtitle}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Stepwise linear model"}\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{autoplot}\NormalTok{(poly\_reg\_house, }\AttributeTok{which =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{ncol =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{ggtitle}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Polynomial model"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{figure}[H]

\begin{minipage}{0.50\linewidth}
\pandocbounded{\includegraphics[keepaspectratio]{10-Regression_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-18-1.pdf}}\end{minipage}%
%
\begin{minipage}{0.50\linewidth}
\pandocbounded{\includegraphics[keepaspectratio]{10-Regression_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-18-2.pdf}}\end{minipage}%

\end{figure}%

}

\caption{\label{fig-ch10-linearity-comparison}Residuals versus fitted
values for the stepwise linear regression model and the polynomial
regression model fitted to the \texttt{house} dataset.}

\end{figure}%

This comparison shows that diagnostic plots are useful not only for
identifying problems but also for guiding model improvement. When
non-linearity is present, possible responses include transforming a
predictor, adding polynomial terms, or adopting a more flexible modeling
approach. In this example, the residual plots support the earlier
decision to include a quadratic term for \texttt{house\_age}.

\subsection*{Non-Constant Variance}\label{non-constant-variance}

A second common problem in regression analysis is non-constant variance,
also known as heteroscedasticity. Linear regression assumes that the
variability of the residuals remains roughly stable across the range of
fitted values. When this assumption does not hold, the residual spread
changes systematically as the fitted values increase or decrease. This
can affect standard errors, confidence intervals, and hypothesis tests,
even when the fitted mean trend appears reasonable.

Two useful diagnostic tools for this issue are the Residuals vs Fitted
plot and the Scale-Location plot. If the constant-variance assumption is
reasonable, the residuals should show a fairly even spread across the
fitted values. A funnel shape, in which the spread widens or narrows as
the fitted values change, suggests heteroscedasticity.

In the \texttt{house} example, the Residuals vs Fitted and
Scale-Location plots do not suggest severe heteroscedasticity. Still, it
is useful to see this problem in a clearer setting. For that reason,
Figure~\ref{fig-ch10-hetero-comparison} presents a simulated example in
which the variance increases with the level of the response. In the left
panel, the residuals from a regression model fitted to the original
response \texttt{y} show a pronounced funnel shape. In the right panel,
the model is refitted using \texttt{log(y)} as the response. The
residual spread is much more even, illustrating how a transformation can
sometimes stabilize variance.

\begin{figure}

\centering{

\begin{figure}[H]

\begin{minipage}{0.50\linewidth}
\pandocbounded{\includegraphics[keepaspectratio]{10-Regression_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-19-1.pdf}}\end{minipage}%
%
\begin{minipage}{0.50\linewidth}
\pandocbounded{\includegraphics[keepaspectratio]{10-Regression_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-19-2.pdf}}\end{minipage}%

\end{figure}%

}

\caption{\label{fig-ch10-hetero-comparison}Residuals versus fitted
values for a simulated example illustrating non-constant variance.}

\end{figure}%

This example shows that heteroscedasticity is often easier to recognize
visually than through a single summary statistic. It also illustrates
one common response: transforming the response variable. In practice,
however, transformations should be guided by the scientific context and
by the scale on which interpretation remains meaningful.

The Scale-Location plot in Figure~\ref{fig-ch10-model-diagnostics}
provides another useful view of this issue by showing how the spread of
standardized residuals changes across the fitted values. Taken together,
these plots help us judge whether the constant-variance assumption is
reasonably satisfied or whether the model may need adjustment.

\subsection*{Non-Normal Residuals}\label{non-normal-residuals}

Another diagnostic concern is whether the residuals are approximately
normally distributed. This issue is less important for estimating the
regression function itself than for inference, since the reliability of
confidence intervals and hypothesis tests depends in part on the
residual distribution, especially when the sample size is small.

A standard tool for assessing this assumption is the Normal Q-Q plot. If
the residuals are approximately normal, the points should lie close to
the diagonal reference line. Systematic departures from that line,
particularly in the tails, may suggest skewness, heavy tails, or other
departures from normality.

As with non-constant variance, it is useful to compare the fitted
\texttt{house} model with a clearer simulated example. In the
\texttt{house} example, the Q-Q plot for the polynomial regression model
does not suggest a serious violation of normality. Still, it is useful
to compare this with a case where the assumption is clearly not
satisfied. For this reason, Figure~\ref{fig-ch10-qq-comparison} shows
two Q-Q plots. The left panel displays the residuals from
\texttt{poly\_reg\_house}, which follow the reference line reasonably
closely. The right panel shows a simulated example with clearly
non-normal residuals. In that case, the pronounced deviation from the
line indicates that the normality assumption is not appropriate.

\begin{figure}

\centering{

\begin{figure}[H]

\begin{minipage}{0.50\linewidth}
\pandocbounded{\includegraphics[keepaspectratio]{10-Regression_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-20-1.pdf}}\end{minipage}%
%
\begin{minipage}{0.50\linewidth}
\pandocbounded{\includegraphics[keepaspectratio]{10-Regression_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-20-2.pdf}}\end{minipage}%

\end{figure}%

}

\caption{\label{fig-ch10-qq-comparison}Normal Q-Q plots for the
polynomial regression model fitted to the \texttt{house} dataset and for
a simulated model with non-normal residuals.}

\end{figure}%

When residuals depart substantially from normality, inferential results
should be interpreted more cautiously. Possible responses include
transforming the response variable, reconsidering the model form, or
using more robust methods when appropriate. The goal is not to force
perfect normality, but to judge whether the departure is mild enough to
tolerate or substantial enough to affect the conclusions.

\subsection*{Influential Observations}\label{influential-observations}

A regression model can also be affected by a small number of influential
observations. These are cases that have the potential to exert
disproportionate influence on the fitted model. In practice, influence
is often greatest when a case combines an unusual position in the
predictor space with a relatively large residual.

The Residuals vs Leverage plot in the lower-right panel of
Figure~\ref{fig-ch10-model-diagnostics} helps identify such cases.
Observations with high leverage lie in unusual regions of the predictor
space. If such observations also have large residuals, they may strongly
affect the estimated coefficients and the fitted regression line. The
Cook's distance contours in this plot provide additional guidance by
indicating which cases may have relatively large influence on the
overall fit.

In the \texttt{house} example, observation 271 stands out because it has
a relatively large residual. This makes it a useful case for closer
inspection. However, a large residual alone does not automatically imply
strong influence. To be influential, a case typically must also have
substantial leverage. The diagnostic plot suggests that no single
observation dominates the fitted model to an extreme degree, although
some deserve additional attention. This distinction matters because
unusual observations are not all equally important for the fitted model.

When potentially influential observations are identified, they should
not be removed automatically. Instead, we should first examine whether
they reflect data entry errors, measurement problems, or unusual but
valid cases. In some situations, an influential observation reveals an
important feature of the data rather than a defect. In others, it may
suggest that the model form should be reconsidered.

\begin{quote}
\emph{Practice:} In the Residuals vs Leverage plot, locate observation
271. Does it stand out mainly because of its residual size, its
leverage, or both?
\end{quote}

Diagnostic analysis is not a separate step performed only after a model
has been fitted. It is part of the modeling process itself. In practice,
we rarely expect a model to satisfy every assumption perfectly. The more
important question is whether any violations are mild enough to tolerate
or serious enough to affect interpretation, inference, or prediction.
For this reason, regression diagnostics help us move beyond mechanical
model fitting toward a more careful and reliable analysis.

\section{Case Study: Predicting Hourly Bike Rental
Demand}\label{sec-ch10-case-bike}

How can we predict how many bikes will be rented in the next hour? For a
bike-sharing system, this is an important practical question. Accurate
short-term predictions can help operators distribute bicycles more
efficiently across stations, anticipate peak demand, and improve service
availability throughout the day.

In this case study, we use regression to model hourly bike rental demand
using weather conditions and time-related information. The analysis is
based on a real-world bike-sharing dataset from Seoul, available in the
\textbf{liver} package. Because the response variable is numerical,
regression provides a natural starting point. At the same time, the
dataset presents several realistic challenges: demand changes sharply
across hours of the day, differs between weekdays and weekends, varies
across seasons, and does not respond linearly to all weather variables.
This makes the dataset a useful setting for showing how regression
models can be strengthened through careful feature construction,
response transformation, and model specification.

We follow the \emph{Data Science Workflow} introduced in Chapter
\ref{sec-ch2-intro-data-science} and illustrated in
Figure~\ref{fig-ch2_DSW}. The analysis also builds on the exploratory
data analysis strategies from Chapter \ref{sec-ch4-EDA} and the data
setup principles from Chapter \ref{sec-ch6-setup-data}. By moving step
by step from understanding the problem and the data to model fitting,
diagnostic checking, evaluation, and interpretation, this case study
shows how regression can support informed prediction of hourly bike
rental demand.

In this setting, hourly demand is shaped by both environmental
conditions and regular daily patterns. For example, demand may increase
during commuting hours, decline late at night, and respond differently
under mild, rainy, or very cold weather conditions. These patterns
suggest that effective prediction will require us to account for both
time-related structure and weather-related effects.

To carry out this case study, we use several R packages. The
\textbf{liver} package provides the dataset, \textbf{dplyr} is used for
data preparation, \textbf{ggplot2} for visualization, \textbf{lubridate}
for working with dates, and \textbf{ggfortify} for regression
diagnostics.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{library}\NormalTok{(liver)      }\CommentTok{\# bike\_demand dataset}
\FunctionTok{library}\NormalTok{(dplyr)      }\CommentTok{\# data manipulation}
\FunctionTok{library}\NormalTok{(ggplot2)    }\CommentTok{\# data visualization}
\FunctionTok{library}\NormalTok{(lubridate)  }\CommentTok{\# date handling}
\FunctionTok{library}\NormalTok{(ggfortify)  }\CommentTok{\# autoplot for regression diagnostics}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\subsection*{Data Understanding, Preparation, and
Exploration}\label{data-understanding-preparation-and-exploration}

We begin by loading the dataset and examining its structure. The
\texttt{bike\_demand} dataset, available in the \textbf{liver} package,
contains hourly records of bike rental demand together with weather and
calendar-related information. Our response variable is
\texttt{bike\_count}, which records the number of rented bikes in each
hour.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{data}\NormalTok{(bike\_demand)}

\FunctionTok{str}\NormalTok{(bike\_demand)}
   \StringTok{\textquotesingle{}data.frame\textquotesingle{}}\SpecialCharTok{:}    \DecValTok{8760}\NormalTok{ obs. of  }\DecValTok{14}\NormalTok{ variables}\SpecialCharTok{:}
    \ErrorTok{$}\NormalTok{ date                 }\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{ chr  }\StringTok{"01/12/2017"} \StringTok{"01/12/2017"} \StringTok{"01/12/2017"} \StringTok{"01/12/2017"}\NormalTok{ ...}
    \SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ hour                 }\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{ int  }\DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{1} \DecValTok{2} \DecValTok{3} \DecValTok{4} \DecValTok{5} \DecValTok{6} \DecValTok{7} \DecValTok{8} \DecValTok{9}\NormalTok{ ...}
    \SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ temperature          }\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{ num  }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{5.2} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{5.5} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{6} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{6.2} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\DecValTok{6} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{6.4} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{6.6} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{7.4} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{7.6} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{6.5}\NormalTok{ ...}
    \SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ humidity             }\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{ int  }\DecValTok{37} \DecValTok{38} \DecValTok{39} \DecValTok{40} \DecValTok{36} \DecValTok{37} \DecValTok{35} \DecValTok{38} \DecValTok{37} \DecValTok{27}\NormalTok{ ...}
    \SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ wind\_speed           }\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{ num  }\FloatTok{2.2} \FloatTok{0.8} \DecValTok{1} \FloatTok{0.9} \FloatTok{2.3} \FloatTok{1.5} \FloatTok{1.3} \FloatTok{0.9} \FloatTok{1.1} \FloatTok{0.5}\NormalTok{ ...}
    \SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ visibility           }\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{ int  }\DecValTok{2000} \DecValTok{2000} \DecValTok{2000} \DecValTok{2000} \DecValTok{2000} \DecValTok{2000} \DecValTok{2000} \DecValTok{2000} \DecValTok{2000} \DecValTok{1928}\NormalTok{ ...}
    \SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ dew\_point\_temperature}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{ num  }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{17.6} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{17.6} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{17.7} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{17.6} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{18.6} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{18.7} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{19.5} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{19.3} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{19.8} \SpecialCharTok{{-}}\FloatTok{22.4}\NormalTok{ ...}
    \SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ solar\_radiation      }\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{ num  }\DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \FloatTok{0.01} \FloatTok{0.23}\NormalTok{ ...}
    \SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ rainfall             }\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{ num  }\DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{ ...}
    \SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ snowfall             }\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{ num  }\DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{ ...}
    \SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ season               }\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{ Factor w}\SpecialCharTok{/} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{ levels }\StringTok{"autumn"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"spring"}\NormalTok{,..}\SpecialCharTok{:} \DecValTok{4} \DecValTok{4} \DecValTok{4} \DecValTok{4} \DecValTok{4} \DecValTok{4} \DecValTok{4} \DecValTok{4} \DecValTok{4} \DecValTok{4}\NormalTok{ ...}
    \SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ holiday              }\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{ Factor w}\SpecialCharTok{/} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ levels }\StringTok{"holiday"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"no holiday"}\SpecialCharTok{:} \DecValTok{2} \DecValTok{2} \DecValTok{2} \DecValTok{2} \DecValTok{2} \DecValTok{2} \DecValTok{2} \DecValTok{2} \DecValTok{2} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ ...}
    \SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ functioning\_day      }\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{ Factor w}\SpecialCharTok{/} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ levels }\StringTok{"no"}\NormalTok{,}\StringTok{"yes"}\SpecialCharTok{:} \DecValTok{2} \DecValTok{2} \DecValTok{2} \DecValTok{2} \DecValTok{2} \DecValTok{2} \DecValTok{2} \DecValTok{2} \DecValTok{2} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ ...}
    \SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{ bike\_count           }\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{ int  }\DecValTok{254} \DecValTok{204} \DecValTok{173} \DecValTok{107} \DecValTok{78} \DecValTok{100} \DecValTok{181} \DecValTok{460} \DecValTok{930} \DecValTok{490}\NormalTok{ ...}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The dataset includes weather-related variables such as
\texttt{temperature}, \texttt{humidity}, \texttt{wind\_speed},
\texttt{visibility}, \texttt{dew\_point\_temperature},
\texttt{solar\_radiation}, \texttt{rainfall}, and \texttt{snowfall}. It
also contains time-related variables such as \texttt{date},
\texttt{hour}, \texttt{season}, \texttt{holiday}, and
\texttt{functioning\_day}. Together, these variables suggest that bike
demand is shaped by both environmental conditions and regular daily or
seasonal patterns.

Before fitting regression models, we prepare the data for analysis.
Since the rental system is unavailable on non-functioning days, we
restrict the analysis to observations where
\texttt{functioning\_day\ ==\ "yes"}. This allows us to focus on
modeling variation in hourly demand when the bike-sharing system is
actually operating.

To begin the preparation, we create a new object called
\texttt{bike\_data} so that the original dataset remains unchanged. We
then convert the \texttt{date} variable to a proper date format and
derive two calendar-based features from it: \texttt{weekday} and
\texttt{month}. The \texttt{weekday} variable helps distinguish between
weekdays and weekends, while \texttt{month} is useful during exploratory
analysis for identifying broader seasonal patterns.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{bike\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ bike\_demand}

\NormalTok{bike\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{date }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{as.Date}\NormalTok{(bike\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{date, }\AttributeTok{format =} \StringTok{"\%d/\%m/\%Y"}\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{bike\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{weekday }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  lubridate}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{wday}\NormalTok{(bike\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{date, }\AttributeTok{label =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{abbr =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{week\_start =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{levels =} \FunctionTok{c}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Mon"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Tue"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Wed"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Thu"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Fri"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Sat"}\NormalTok{, }\StringTok{"Sun"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{)}

\NormalTok{bike\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{month }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  lubridate}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{month}\NormalTok{(bike\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{date, }\AttributeTok{label =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{abbr =} \ConstantTok{TRUE}\NormalTok{),}
  \AttributeTok{levels =}\NormalTok{ month.abb}
\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Next, we keep only the observations recorded during functioning periods.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{bike\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{filter}\NormalTok{(bike\_data, functioning\_day }\SpecialCharTok{==} \StringTok{"yes"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

We then sort the observations chronologically by \texttt{date} and
\texttt{hour}. This is important because later we will divide the data
into training and test sets based on time, so the rows must be ordered
from earlier to later observations.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{bike\_data }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ dplyr}\SpecialCharTok{::}\FunctionTok{arrange}\NormalTok{(bike\_data, date, hour)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Finally, we convert \texttt{hour} to a factor. Bike demand is unlikely
to change in a simple linear way over the course of the day. For
example, demand may increase during commuting hours and decrease late at
night. Treating \texttt{hour} as a categorical variable allows the
regression model to estimate a separate effect for each hour.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{bike\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{hour }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{factor}\NormalTok{(bike\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{hour, }\AttributeTok{levels =} \DecValTok{0}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{23}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

We now examine the distribution of the response variable. The
distribution of \texttt{bike\_count} is right-skewed, with many moderate
values and a smaller number of much larger counts. Such a pattern
suggests that modeling the response directly may lead to non-constant
variance and a less stable regression fit. To assess whether a simple
transformation improves the shape of the distribution, we compare
\texttt{bike\_count} with its square-root transformation.

\begin{figure}

\centering{

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(bike\_data, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ bike\_count)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_histogram}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{bins =} \DecValTok{30}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Hourly bike rentals"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Frequency"}\NormalTok{)}

\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(bike\_data, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =} \FunctionTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(bike\_count))) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_histogram}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{bins =} \DecValTok{30}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Square{-}root transformed rentals"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Frequency"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{figure}[H]

\begin{minipage}{0.50\linewidth}
\pandocbounded{\includegraphics[keepaspectratio]{10-Regression_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-27-1.pdf}}\end{minipage}%
%
\begin{minipage}{0.50\linewidth}
\pandocbounded{\includegraphics[keepaspectratio]{10-Regression_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-27-2.pdf}}\end{minipage}%

\end{figure}%

}

\caption{\label{fig-ch10-bike-response-distribution}Distribution of
hourly bike rentals before (left) and after the square-root
transformation (right).}

\end{figure}%

Figure Figure~\ref{fig-ch10-bike-response-distribution} shows that
\texttt{bike\_count} is clearly right-skewed, with many relatively small
or moderate values and a smaller number of much larger counts. The
distribution is not close to symmetric. In contrast, the square-root
transformation reduces the skewness and compresses the range of large
values, so that the transformed response appears more symmetric and more
nearly bell-shaped around its center. This provides a practical reason
to model bike demand on the transformed scale. We therefore create a new
response variable, \texttt{sqrt\_bike\_count}, which will be used in the
regression models that follow.

\begin{quote}
\emph{Practice:} Try an alternative transformation of
\texttt{bike\_count}, such as \texttt{log(bike\_count\ +\ 1)}, and
compare the resulting distribution with the histograms in Figure
Figure~\ref{fig-ch10-bike-response-distribution}. Does this alternative
appear more suitable than the square-root transformation for this
dataset?
\end{quote}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{bike\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{sqrt\_bike\_count }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(bike\_data}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{bike\_count)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

To better understand the daily pattern of bike use, we next examine how
average demand changes across the hours of the day.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(bike\_data, }\FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ hour, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ bike\_count, }\AttributeTok{group =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{stat\_summary}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{fun =}\NormalTok{ mean, }\AttributeTok{geom =} \StringTok{"line"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linewidth =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{stat\_summary}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{fun =}\NormalTok{ mean, }\AttributeTok{geom =} \StringTok{"point"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{size =} \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Hour of day"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Average bike rentals"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{figure}[H]

\centering{

\includegraphics[width=0.75\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{10-Regression_files/figure-pdf/fig-ch10-bike-hourly-demand-1.pdf}

}

\caption{\label{fig-ch10-bike-hourly-demand}Average hourly bike demand
on functioning days.}

\end{figure}%

Figure Figure~\ref{fig-ch10-bike-hourly-demand} shows that bike demand
does not vary linearly over the course of the day. Instead, it follows a
clear daily pattern, with two pronounced peaks around 08:00 and 18:00.
These peaks may reflect commuting behavior, as bike use often increases
at the beginning and end of the working day. Demand is much lower during
late-night and early-morning periods, then rises again as daily activity
begins. This pattern suggests that the effect of \texttt{hour} is not
well represented by a single straight-line trend. For this reason, we
treat \texttt{hour} as a factor rather than as a numeric predictor,
allowing the regression model to estimate a separate effect for each
hour of the day.

\begin{quote}
\emph{Practice:} Look at Figure
Figure~\ref{fig-ch10-bike-hourly-demand}. Which hours appear to have the
highest average demand, and why does this pattern suggest that
\texttt{hour} should be treated as a factor rather than as a numeric
predictor?
\end{quote}

Although we created both \texttt{weekday} and \texttt{month}, they will
not play the same role later in the analysis. The \texttt{weekday}
variable will be used in the final regression models because differences
between weekdays and weekends are directly relevant for prediction. The
\texttt{month} variable is useful during exploratory analysis, but we do
not include it in the final regression models. Because the data will
later be split chronologically, the final portion of the year forms the
test set, and using \texttt{month} as a factor could introduce levels in
the test data that are not represented in the training data.

\subsection*{Data Setup for Modeling}\label{data-setup-for-modeling-5}

Because the observations are ordered over time, we evaluate the model
using future observations rather than a random sample from the full
dataset. In this setting, a random train-test split would mix earlier
and later time points, which would not reflect the practical goal of
predicting future bike demand from past data. Instead, we use the first
80\% of the observations as the training set and the remaining 20\% as
the test set.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{n\_total }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{nrow}\NormalTok{(bike\_data)}
\NormalTok{n\_train }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{floor}\NormalTok{(}\FloatTok{0.8} \SpecialCharTok{*}\NormalTok{ n\_total)}

\NormalTok{train\_bike }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ bike\_data[}\DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{n\_train, ]}
\NormalTok{test\_bike  }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}}\NormalTok{ bike\_data[(n\_train }\SpecialCharTok{+} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{)}\SpecialCharTok{:}\NormalTok{n\_total, ]}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

This split is more realistic for a time-ordered dataset because it
respects the chronological structure of the observations. It also
reduces the risk of an overly optimistic performance estimate that could
arise if nearby time points were randomly assigned to both the training
and test sets.

\begin{quote}
\emph{Practice:} Create a 70\%-30\% chronological split of the data.
Compare the training and test sets in terms of the distribution of
\texttt{bike\_count}, and reflect on how changing the split may affect
the difficulty of the prediction task.
\end{quote}

\subsection*{Fitting Regression Models with a Transformed
Response}\label{fitting-regression-models-with-a-transformed-response}

We now fit regression models using \texttt{sqrt\_bike\_count} as the
response. As the exploratory analysis showed, the square-root
transformation makes the response distribution more symmetric and
reduces the influence of very large rental counts. We also treat
\texttt{hour} as a factor, since the daily demand pattern showed clear
peaks and troughs rather than a simple linear trend over the course of
the day.

We begin with a full regression model. It includes the weather-related
predictors, the categorical variables \texttt{season} and
\texttt{holiday}, the derived \texttt{weekday} feature, and
\texttt{hour} as a factor. In addition, we include a quadratic term for
temperature through \texttt{I(temperature\^{}2)}, allowing the model to
capture a curved relationship between temperature and bike demand. This
is plausible in practice, since both very cold and very warm conditions
may reduce ridership. Although we created \texttt{month} during data
preparation, we do not include it here because the data will be
evaluated using a chronological split, and some month levels in the test
period may not be represented in the training period.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{full\_reg }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{lm}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  sqrt\_bike\_count }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{ temperature }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{I}\NormalTok{(temperature}\SpecialCharTok{\^{}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ humidity }\SpecialCharTok{+}
\NormalTok{    wind\_speed }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ visibility }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ dew\_point\_temperature }\SpecialCharTok{+}
\NormalTok{    solar\_radiation }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ rainfall }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ snowfall }\SpecialCharTok{+}
\NormalTok{    season }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ holiday }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ hour }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ weekday,}
  \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ train\_bike}
\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

Next, we fit a second regression model using stepwise selection based on
the AIC criterion. Starting from the full model, this procedure searches
for a simpler set of predictors by balancing goodness of fit against
model complexity. Stepwise regression should not replace subject-matter
reasoning, but it is useful here as a practical comparison to assess
whether a slightly simpler model performs similarly to the full one.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{stepwise\_reg }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{step}\NormalTok{(full\_reg, }\AttributeTok{direction =} \StringTok{"both"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{trace =} \ConstantTok{FALSE}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

To see which predictors are retained, we inspect the formula of the
stepwise model.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{formula}\NormalTok{(stepwise\_reg)}
\NormalTok{   sqrt\_bike\_count }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{ temperature }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{I}\NormalTok{(temperature}\SpecialCharTok{\^{}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ humidity }\SpecialCharTok{+} 
\NormalTok{       visibility }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ dew\_point\_temperature }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ solar\_radiation }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ rainfall }\SpecialCharTok{+} 
\NormalTok{       snowfall }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ season }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ holiday }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ hour }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ weekday}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The resulting formula shows that only one predictor was removed:
\texttt{wind\_speed}. This suggests that, after accounting for the other
predictors, \texttt{wind\_speed} did not improve the model enough to be
retained under the AIC criterion. All other predictors from the full
model remained in the stepwise specification.

\subsubsection*{Diagnostic Checking}\label{diagnostic-checking}

Before comparing predictive performance, we examine whether the fitted
regression model shows any clear diagnostic problems. A model may
perform reasonably well in prediction while still showing residual
patterns that suggest misspecification. We therefore inspect two
standard diagnostic plots for the stepwise model: the Residuals vs
Fitted plot and the Normal Q-Q plot.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{autoplot}\NormalTok{(stepwise\_reg, }\AttributeTok{which =} \DecValTok{1}\SpecialCharTok{:}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{ggtitle}\NormalTok{(}\StringTok{"Stepwise model"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=1\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{10-Regression_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-33-1.pdf}
\end{center}

These plots help us assess whether the model fits the data reasonably
well. In the Residuals vs Fitted plot, we look for residuals that are
centered around zero without a strong systematic pattern. A visible
curve would suggest remaining nonlinearity, while a funnel shape would
suggest non-constant variance. In the Normal Q-Q plot, we assess whether
the residuals are approximately normally distributed. Small departures
from the reference line are not unusual, especially in a dataset of this
size, but clear deviations in the tails deserve closer attention.

In this case, the residuals appear to be reasonably centered around
zero, with no strong evidence of a pronounced curve or funnel shape. The
Q-Q plot shows one clear point in the upper tail, corresponding to
observation 3950 in the training set. This observation is not
necessarily unusual in terms of its raw bike count, but it has a
relatively large residual and therefore contributes to a departure from
normality in the residual distribution. In a dataset of this size, a
single such point is not necessarily a major concern, although it is
still useful to inspect it more closely.

\begin{quote}
\emph{Practice:} Examine observation 3950 in the training set by running
\texttt{train\_bike{[}3950,\ {]}}. Does it appear to be a data error, or
does it look like a plausible but unusual hour of bike demand? What
effect might such an observation have on the fitted regression model?
\end{quote}

The corresponding diagnostic plots for the full regression model are
very similar and lead to the same overall conclusion. For that reason,
we do not reproduce them here. Curious readers may generate the same
plots for \texttt{full\_reg} and compare the two models directly.

\begin{quote}
\emph{Practice:} Generate the diagnostic plots for \texttt{full\_reg}
and compare them with those of the stepwise model. Do the two models
differ meaningfully in terms of residual pattern or normality?
\end{quote}

\subsection*{Model Evaluation and
Comparison}\label{model-evaluation-and-comparison}

Because the response variable is numerical, we evaluate the fitted
models using regression metrics. In particular, we use RMSE and \(R^2\)
to compare the predictive performance of the full and stepwise models on
the test set. As discussed in Chapter \ref{sec-ch8-regression-metrics},
these metrics summarize how closely the predicted values match the
observed outcomes. We also use the Residual Standard Error (RSE) and
adjusted \(R^2\) to compare the fitted models on the training data, and
we complement the numerical summary with a plot of observed versus
predicted bike rentals for the stepwise model.

We begin by comparing the fitted models on the training data using the
Residual Standard Error and adjusted \(R^2\).

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{residuals}\NormalTok{(full\_reg)}\SpecialCharTok{\^{}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{df.residual}\NormalTok{(full\_reg))}
\NormalTok{   [}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\FloatTok{6.120529}
\FunctionTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{residuals}\NormalTok{(stepwise\_reg)}\SpecialCharTok{\^{}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{df.residual}\NormalTok{(stepwise\_reg))}
\NormalTok{   [}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\FloatTok{6.120677}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{summary}\NormalTok{(full\_reg)}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{adj.r.squared}
\NormalTok{   [}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\FloatTok{0.7377221}
\FunctionTok{summary}\NormalTok{(stepwise\_reg)}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{adj.r.squared}
\NormalTok{   [}\DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{] }\FloatTok{0.7377094}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The RSE and adjusted \(R^2\) values for the full and stepwise models are
very close to each other. This suggests that both models fit the
training data similarly. The fact that the stepwise model achieves
nearly the same fit after removing \texttt{wind\_speed} supports the
idea that the simpler specification may be sufficient.

To assess predictive performance more directly, however, we must
evaluate the models on the test set. Since the models were trained on
\texttt{sqrt\_bike\_count}, we first generate predictions on the
transformed scale and then convert them back to the original scale by
squaring them. Because a regression model on the square-root scale can
occasionally produce slightly negative predictions, we truncate such
values at zero before squaring.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{pred\_full\_sqrt }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{predict}\NormalTok{(full\_reg, }\AttributeTok{newdata =}\NormalTok{ test\_bike)}
\NormalTok{pred\_stepwise\_sqrt }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{predict}\NormalTok{(stepwise\_reg, }\AttributeTok{newdata =}\NormalTok{ test\_bike)}

\NormalTok{pred\_full }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{pmax}\NormalTok{(pred\_full\_sqrt, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)}\SpecialCharTok{\^{}}\DecValTok{2}
\NormalTok{pred\_stepwise }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \FunctionTok{pmax}\NormalTok{(pred\_stepwise\_sqrt, }\DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{)}\SpecialCharTok{\^{}}\DecValTok{2}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

We now compute RMSE and \(R^2\) on the original scale of hourly bike
rentals.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{reg\_metrics }\OtherTok{\textless{}{-}} \ControlFlowTok{function}\NormalTok{(actual, predicted, model) \{}
  \FunctionTok{data.frame}\NormalTok{(}
    \AttributeTok{Model =}\NormalTok{ model,}
    \AttributeTok{RMSE =} \FunctionTok{sqrt}\NormalTok{(}\FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{((actual }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ predicted)}\SpecialCharTok{\^{}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)),}
    \AttributeTok{R2 =} \DecValTok{1} \SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{((actual }\SpecialCharTok{{-}}\NormalTok{ predicted)}\SpecialCharTok{\^{}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{/} \FunctionTok{sum}\NormalTok{((actual }\SpecialCharTok{{-}} \FunctionTok{mean}\NormalTok{(actual))}\SpecialCharTok{\^{}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{  )}
\NormalTok{\}}

\FunctionTok{rbind}\NormalTok{(}
  \FunctionTok{reg\_metrics}\NormalTok{(test\_bike}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{bike\_count, pred\_full, }\StringTok{"Full model"}\NormalTok{),}
  \FunctionTok{reg\_metrics}\NormalTok{(test\_bike}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{bike\_count, pred\_stepwise, }\StringTok{"Stepwise model"}\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{)}
\NormalTok{              Model     RMSE        R2}
   \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{     Full model }\FloatTok{335.2158} \FloatTok{0.6871619}
   \DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{ Stepwise model }\FloatTok{333.8172} \FloatTok{0.6897669}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

The test-set RMSE and \(R^2\) values for the full and stepwise models
are also very close, indicating that the two models have nearly the same
predictive performance on unseen data. At the same time, the stepwise
model performs slightly better, with a slightly lower RMSE and a
slightly higher \(R^2\). This suggests that removing
\texttt{wind\_speed} does not reduce predictive accuracy and may even
improve it marginally. Taken together, these results support the
stepwise model as the preferred specification, since it is slightly
simpler while performing at least as well as the full model.

To complement the numerical evaluation, we also plot the observed and
predicted bike rentals for the stepwise model on the test set.

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{ggplot}\NormalTok{(}
  \FunctionTok{data.frame}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{observed =}\NormalTok{ test\_bike}\SpecialCharTok{$}\NormalTok{bike\_count, }\AttributeTok{predicted =}\NormalTok{ pred\_stepwise),}
  \FunctionTok{aes}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =}\NormalTok{ observed, }\AttributeTok{y =}\NormalTok{ predicted)}
\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_point}\NormalTok{() }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{geom\_abline}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{intercept =} \DecValTok{0}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{slope =} \DecValTok{1}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{linetype =} \StringTok{"dashed"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{colour =} \StringTok{"firebrick3"}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}
  \FunctionTok{labs}\NormalTok{(}\AttributeTok{x =} \StringTok{"Observed bike rentals"}\NormalTok{, }\AttributeTok{y =} \StringTok{"Predicted bike rentals"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{center}
\includegraphics[width=0.65\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio]{10-Regression_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-38-1.pdf}
\end{center}

The points lie reasonably close to the diagonal reference line,
especially for observed bike counts below about 1500, which suggests
that the stepwise model predicts moderate demand levels fairly well. For
larger observed values, however, the points tend to fall below the
diagonal, indicating that the model slightly underpredicts the
highest-demand hours. This pattern suggests that the model captures the
overall structure of bike demand well, while still having more
difficulty with extreme demand levels.

This case study illustrates an important lesson about regression
modeling. Predictive performance does not depend only on the choice of
method. It also depends on how thoughtfully we represent the structure
of the data. By using a transformed response, treating \texttt{hour} as
a factor, respecting the temporal ordering of the observations, and
allowing a nonlinear effect for temperature, we obtain a regression
model that is both interpretable and practically useful.

\begin{quote}
\emph{Practice:} Repeat the analysis using a 70\% training set and a
30\% test set, again preserving the chronological order of the data.
Compute RMSE and \(R^2\) for both models on the new test set, and
compare the results with those obtained from the 80\%-20\% split. Does
changing the partition alter which model appears preferable?
\end{quote}

\section{Chapter Summary and Takeaways}\label{sec-ch10-summary}

In this chapter, we introduced regression analysis as a core framework
for modeling continuous outcomes. We began with simple linear regression
and then extended the discussion to multiple regression, showing how
additional predictors can improve our ability to explain variation in a
response variable. Along the way, we emphasized that regression serves
several purposes at once: it can be used for prediction, for
interpretation, and for statistical inference about the role of
individual predictors.

We also examined how to evaluate regression models from several
complementary perspectives. Measures such as the residual standard
error, \(R^2\), and adjusted \(R^2\) help us assess model fit, while
hypothesis tests help us judge whether predictors contribute information
beyond what is explained by the others. At the same time, we saw that
good numerical fit alone is not enough: a useful regression model should
also be interpretable, appropriately specified, and well aligned with
the structure of the data.

To move beyond strictly linear relationships, we introduced polynomial
regression as a practical way to model curvature while remaining within
the familiar linear modeling framework. We also examined stepwise
regression as a tool for comparing alternative predictor sets and
selecting a more parsimonious model. Throughout the chapter, we stressed
that these modeling choices should be guided not only by summary
statistics, but also by diagnostic checks that reveal whether important
assumptions appear reasonable.

The bike rental case study brought these ideas together in a realistic
setting. There, we showed how response transformation, feature
construction, time-aware data partitioning, model comparison, and
diagnostic analysis all contribute to effective regression modeling in
practice. The broader lesson is that successful modeling does not come
from choosing the most complicated method available, but from choosing a
model that is appropriate for the question, the data, and the intended
use of the results.

This chapter therefore reinforces an important principle of the Data
Science Workflow: reliable modeling requires both statistical tools and
careful judgment. Regression models provide a structured way to quantify
relationships, assess uncertainty, and generate interpretable
predictions when the response is continuous. In the next chapter, we
extend these ideas to situations in which the response is no longer
continuous, turning to generalized regression models for binary and
count outcomes.

\section{Exercises}\label{sec-ch10-exercises}

These exercises reinforce the main ideas of the chapter by combining
conceptual understanding, interpretation of regression output, model
comparison, diagnostic reasoning, and practical implementation in R. The
hands-on sections use the \texttt{house}, \texttt{insurance}, and
\texttt{bike\_demand} datasets from the \textbf{liver} package.

\subsection*{Conceptual Questions}\label{conceptual-questions-7}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\item
  In a simple linear regression model, how should we interpret the
  intercept and the slope?
\item
  What does the \(R^2\) value tell us about a regression model?
\item
  Why is adjusted \(R^2\) often more informative than \(R^2\) when
  comparing models with different numbers of predictors?
\item
  What does the residual standard error measure, and how does it differ
  from \(R^2\)?
\item
  Why is it useful to distinguish between interpretation and prediction
  when evaluating a regression model?
\item
  What is multicollinearity, and why can it make regression coefficients
  harder to interpret?
\item
  What is the purpose of stepwise regression, and what are its
  limitations?
\item
  Why is polynomial regression still considered a linear model?
\item
  What kinds of patterns in a Residuals vs Fitted plot might suggest
  that a linear model is inadequate?
\item
  Why should regression diagnostics be viewed as part of model building
  rather than as a final checklist applied after fitting the model?
\end{enumerate}

\subsection*{\texorpdfstring{Hands-On Practice: Regression Analysis with
with
\texttt{house}}{Hands-On Practice: Regression Analysis with with house}}\label{hands-on-practice-regression-analysis-with-with-house}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{data}\NormalTok{(house, }\AttributeTok{package =} \StringTok{"liver"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\setcounter{enumi}{10}
\item
  Use the \texttt{partition()} function introduced earlier in the book
  to split the \texttt{house} data into training and test sets. Use 80\%
  of the observations for training and 20\% for testing, and store the
  results as \texttt{train\_data} and \texttt{test\_data}.
\item
  Fit the full regression model on the training data and store it as
  \texttt{full\_model\_train}:
\end{enumerate}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{full\_model\_train }\OtherTok{=} \FunctionTok{lm}\NormalTok{(unit\_price }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{ ., }\AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ train\_data)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\setcounter{enumi}{12}
\item
  Apply stepwise regression to \texttt{full\_model\_train} and store the
  result as \texttt{stepwise\_model}. Which predictor or predictors are
  removed?
\item
  Fit the polynomial regression model below using the training data:
\end{enumerate}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\NormalTok{poly\_reg\_house }\OtherTok{=} \FunctionTok{lm}\NormalTok{(}
\NormalTok{  unit\_price }\SpecialCharTok{\textasciitilde{}}\NormalTok{ house\_age }\SpecialCharTok{+} \FunctionTok{I}\NormalTok{(house\_age}\SpecialCharTok{\^{}}\DecValTok{2}\NormalTok{) }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ distance\_to\_MRT }\SpecialCharTok{+}
\NormalTok{    stores\_number }\SpecialCharTok{+}\NormalTok{ latitude,}
  \AttributeTok{data =}\NormalTok{ train\_data}
\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\setcounter{enumi}{14}
\item
  Compare \texttt{full\_model\_train}, \texttt{stepwise\_model}, and
  \texttt{poly\_reg\_house} using the residual standard error and
  adjusted \(R^2\) on the training data. What do these measures suggest,
  and what do they not tell you about performance on unseen data?
\item
  Use the three fitted models to predict \texttt{unit\_price} for the
  test set. Compute the mean squared error (MSE) for each model. Which
  model performs best on unseen data?
\item
  Compare the test-set MSE of \texttt{stepwise\_model} and
  \texttt{poly\_reg\_house}. Does allowing curvature in
  \texttt{house\_age} improve predictive performance enough to justify
  the added complexity?
\item
  Create a plot showing the relationship between \texttt{house\_age} and
  \texttt{unit\_price}, and add fitted curves from both a linear and a
  quadratic model based only on \texttt{house\_age}. What does this
  comparison suggest about the need for a nonlinear term?
\item
  Compare the Residuals vs Fitted plots of \texttt{stepwise\_model} and
  \texttt{poly\_reg\_house}. Which model shows less evidence of
  non-linearity?
\item
  Examine the Normal Q-Q, Scale-Location, and Residuals vs Leverage
  plots for \texttt{poly\_reg\_house}. Do the diagnostics suggest any
  serious problems?
\item
  Observation 271 was highlighted in the chapter. Does it still stand
  out in the diagnostic plots for \texttt{poly\_reg\_house} fitted on
  the training data? What can you say about its residual size, leverage,
  and possible influence?
\item
  Extend \texttt{poly\_reg\_house} by adding a cubic term,
  \texttt{I(house\_age\^{}3)}. Compare the cubic model with the
  quadratic model using both adjusted \(R^2\) on the training data and
  MSE on the test data. Does the cubic term appear to improve the model
  enough to justify the extra complexity?
\item
  Based on your comparisons, which of the three models would you
  recommend if the goal is prediction, and which would you recommend if
  the goal is interpretation? Explain your reasoning.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection*{\texorpdfstring{Hands-On Practice: Regression Analysis with
\texttt{insurance}}{Hands-On Practice: Regression Analysis with insurance}}\label{hands-on-practice-regression-analysis-with-insurance}

\begin{Shaded}
\begin{Highlighting}[]
\FunctionTok{data}\NormalTok{(insurance, }\AttributeTok{package =} \StringTok{"liver"}\NormalTok{)}
\end{Highlighting}
\end{Shaded}

\begin{enumerate}
\def\labelenumi{\arabic{enumi}.}
\setcounter{enumi}{23}
\item
  Inspect the structure of the \texttt{insurance} dataset. Which
